title: Practice Midterm Q4 — Pareto-Undominated Points / 2D Maxima (10 pts)
source: Practice Midterm, Part B (from last year's midterm)
statement:
A set $S$ of $n$ points $(x_i, y_i)$ in the plane, with all $x_i$ distinct and all $y_i$ distinct. Point $i$ **dominates** $j$ if $x_i > x_j$ AND $y_i > y_j$. A point is **undominated** if no other point dominates it.

(a) List undominated points in a given diagram.
(b) Draw a 5-point set where all 5 are undominated; another where only 1 is undominated.
(c) Design the most efficient algorithm you can. Hint: divide and conquer, or a simple sorting approach.
(d) Asymptotic worst-case runtime; state recurrence if D&C.

steps.title: (a) Reading undominated points off the diagram.
steps.body: A point is undominated iff nothing is strictly above-and-right of it. In the given figure, the undominated set (the 'Pareto frontier') is the staircase from the top-left going down and to the right: $\{2, 10, 12, 13\}$. (Point 2 is highest-$y$; each subsequent point is strictly right and strictly lower than the previous, so none dominates another.)

steps.title: (b) Extreme cases.
steps.body:
**All 5 undominated:** put the 5 points on a strictly decreasing staircase — as $x$ increases, $y$ decreases. E.g. $(1,5), (2,4), (3,3), (4,2), (5,1)$. No point dominates another.

**Only 1 undominated:** put 1 'champion' point in the top-right; the other 4 somewhere below-left of it. E.g. champion $(5,5)$, others $(1,1), (2,2), (3,3), (4,4)$. The champion dominates all others.

steps.title: (c) Sort-then-scan algorithm (the clean option).
steps.body:
**Algorithm.**

1. Sort the $n$ points by $x$-coordinate in descending order. Let the sorted list be $p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_n$ (so $p_1$ has the largest $x$).
2. Scan left-to-right through the sorted list, maintaining a running $\max_y$.
3. Emit $p_i$ as undominated iff $y_i > \max_y$ so far, and update $\max_y \leftarrow y_i$.

**Why correct.** In the sorted order, every point scanned before $p_i$ has strictly larger $x$. So $p_i$ is dominated iff some earlier point also has larger $y$ — equivalently, $y_i \le \max_y$ among the earlier points. The scan checks exactly this.

**Runtime.** Sorting is $O(n \log n)$. The scan is $O(n)$. Total $O(n \log n)$.

tips.label: Why this is simpler than D&C
tips.content: Sorting already does the heavy lifting. Once sorted by $x$, 'dominated' reduces to 'some earlier element has larger $y$', which a single pass computes.

steps.title: (c-alt) Divide-and-conquer algorithm.
steps.body:
**Algorithm.**

1. **Base case.** If $|S| \le 1$, return $S$ (singletons are undominated).
2. Split $S$ by the median $x$-coordinate into left half $L$ (smaller $x$) and right half $R$ (larger $x$).
3. Recursively compute $U_L = $ undominated in $L$, $U_R = $ undominated in $R$.
4. **Combine.** Every point in $U_R$ has larger $x$ than every point in $L$, so no point in $U_R$ can be dominated by anything in $L$. So all of $U_R$ survives.

For $U_L$: a point $p \in U_L$ is dominated globally iff some point in $R$ has both larger $x$ and larger $y$. Every point in $R$ has larger $x$. So $p$ is globally undominated iff $y_p > y_{\max}(R)$, where $y_{\max}(R)$ is the max $y$-coordinate among all points in $R$.

Compute $y_{\max}(R)$ in $O(n)$. Filter $U_L$ accordingly in $O(n)$.

5. Return $\{p \in U_L : y_p > y_{\max}(R)\} \cup U_R$.

**Why correct.** A point is undominated globally iff (i) it is undominated within its own half, AND (ii) nothing in the other half dominates it. For $U_R$: there's no other half to check (all of $L$ has smaller $x$ → cannot dominate $R$-points). For $U_L$: the $x$-coordinate of any $R$-point is already larger; domination hinges only on $y$.

**Recurrence.** $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(n) = \Theta(n \log n)$.

tips.label: Split is cheap once sorted
tips.content: If you sort by $x$ once at the start (outside recursion), the 'split by median $x$' is an $O(1)$ index lookup. Each recursive call doesn't need to re-sort.

steps.title: (d) Runtime summary.
steps.body:
**Sort-then-scan:** $O(n \log n)$ for the initial sort + $O(n)$ linear pass = $\Theta(n \log n)$.

**D&C:** recurrence $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(n)$. By recursion tree (same as Merge Sort), $T(n) = \Theta(n \log n)$.

Both achieve $\Theta(n \log n)$. The sorting-based approach is usually cleaner to write up.

tips.label: Can we do better?
tips.content: No (in the comparison model). A lower-bound argument via reduction from sorting: if you could output the undominated set sorted by $x$ in $o(n \log n)$, you could sort real numbers in $o(n \log n)$, contradicting the comparison-based sorting lower bound.

answerKey: Sort by $x$, scan maintaining max $y$. $\Theta(n \log n)$. Or D&C: split by median $x$, recurse, filter left half by $y_{\max}$ of right. Recurrence $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(n) = \Theta(n \log n)$.
